claim: Tofersen reduces CSF SOD1 by ~35% and plasma NfL by ~60% in SOD1-ALS over 28 weeks
notes: VALOR RCT, n=108. Primary endpoint (ALSFRS-R) not met at 28wk, but biomarker endpoints strongly positive. Led to accelerated approval based on SOD1 reduction as surrogate.

claim: Early tofersen initiation associated with ~25% stabilization advantage over delayed start at 3.5 years in SOD1-ALS open-label extension
notes: JAMA Neurology 2025. OLE of VALOR trial. Early-start group showed sustained benefit in ALSFRS-R, respiratory function, and survival vs delayed-start. Strongest signal for presymptomatic/early intervention.

claim: AAV-mediated SOD1 gene silencing achieves ~25% slowing of progression at 1 year in SOD1-ALS
notes: Approved 2025 under accelerated pathway. One-time intrathecal gene therapy. Durable SOD1 suppression without repeated lumbar punctures. Complementary to tofersen approach.

claim: SOD1 mutations cause ALS through toxic gain-of-function in transgenic mice (G93A model), establishing gene-to-disease causality
notes: Gurney et al 1994, Science. SOD1-G93A transgenic mouse developed progressive motor neuron disease despite retaining dismutase activity. Landmark paper establishing toxic gain-of-function mechanism.

claim: BIIB078 ASO targeting C9orf72 repeat RNA failed to reduce DPR proteins or pTDP-43 despite adequate CNS distribution
notes: Bhatt et al, Cell 2025. Phase 1 trial. Post-mortem analysis showed ASO reached target tissue but DPRs and pTDP-43 persisted. Suggests repeat RNA knockdown alone insufficient — downstream pathology self-sustaining.

claim: GGGGCC hexanucleotide repeat expansion in C9orf72 is the most common genetic cause of ALS and FTD
notes: DeJesus-Hernandez et al 2011, Neuron. Discovery of C9orf72 repeat expansion. Accounts for ~40% familial ALS and ~25% familial FTD. Also found in ~7% sporadic ALS.

claim: C9orf72 repeat RNA undergoes repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation producing five dipeptide repeat proteins (DPRs) that are toxic to neurons
notes: Mori et al 2013, Science. Identified all 5 DPR species (poly-GA, poly-GP, poly-GR, poly-PA, poly-PR). Poly-GR and poly-PR most toxic. Established RAN translation as key pathogenic mechanism.

claim: C9orf72 loss-of-function causes immune dysregulation and neuroinflammation, contributing to disease alongside toxic gain-of-function
notes: O'Rourke et al 2016, Science. C9orf72 null mice develop splenomegaly, lymphadenopathy, and autoimmune features. Suggests C9orf72 repeat expansion has dual mechanism: GOF (DPR toxicity) + LOF (immune dysregulation).

claim: Jacifusen (ulefnersen) reduces plasma NfL by 82.8% in expanded access FUS-ALS patients over 12 months
notes: Ionis/Jacifusen Foundation expanded access program, 12 patients. Dramatic NfL reduction. Supported by n-of-1 compassionate use case in Jaci Hermstad that inspired program name.

claim: FUS protein undergoes liquid-liquid phase separation that can convert to solid-like pathological aggregates, explaining FUS-ALS toxicity
notes: Patel et al 2015, Cell. Demonstrated that FUS liquid droplets mature into solid fibrillar aggregates over time or with ALS mutations. Disease mutations accelerate liquid-to-solid transition.

claim: FUSION Phase 3 trial of jacifusen enrolling 80+ FUS-ALS patients, the largest FUS-ALS trial ever conducted
notes: Phase 3 pivotal trial. Rare disease challenge: FUS mutations account for ~3% familial ALS. Multi-center global enrollment. Primary endpoint ALSFRS-R slope.

claim: VTx-002 TDP-43 intrabody gene therapy received FDA Fast Track designation and is entering PIONEER-ALS Phase 1/2 trial
notes: Voyager Therapeutics. First therapy directly targeting TDP-43 aggregation in sporadic ALS. Intrabody delivered via AAV binds cytoplasmic TDP-43 aggregates. HIGHLY RELEVANT for Erik (sporadic, likely TDP-43).

claim: TDP-43 is the major disease protein in ALS, found in cytoplasmic inclusions in ~97% of all ALS cases
notes: Neumann et al 2006, Science. Discovery of TDP-43 as ubiquitinated inclusion protein in ALS and FTLD. Landmark paper establishing TDP-43 proteinopathy as unifying feature of most ALS.

claim: TDP-43 nuclear loss causes incorporation of cryptic exons in multiple transcripts, disrupting essential neuronal proteins
notes: Ling et al 2015, Science. TDP-43 normally represses cryptic exon inclusion. Nuclear depletion (as in ALS) de-represses hundreds of cryptic exons. Key downstream targets: STMN2, UNC13A.

claim: TDP-43 nuclear clearance with cytoplasmic aggregation is the defining pathological hallmark of ALS, present in 97% of cases
notes: Nuclear-to-cytoplasmic mislocalization is both loss-of-function (nuclear RNA processing) and gain-of-function (cytoplasmic aggregation toxicity). Dual mechanism drives disease.

claim: AP-2 complex modulates TDP-43 nuclear-cytoplasmic balance; AP-2alpha restoration reduces TDP-43 mislocalization in preclinical models
notes: Molefy/academic collaboration. AP-2 adaptor complex involved in TDP-43 nuclear import. Preclinical restoration of AP-2alpha reduces cytoplasmic TDP-43 aggregation. Early-stage but novel mechanism.

claim: Genetic testing is recommended for all ALS patients regardless of family history, as ~10% of apparently sporadic ALS has identifiable genetic cause
notes: AAN/ACMG practice guideline 2023. Recommends genetic testing for all ALS patients. C9orf72 most common finding in sporadic cases. Results increasingly actionable (tofersen for SOD1, jacifusen for FUS). CRITICAL for Erik — genetics PENDING.

claim: Gold Coast criteria simplify ALS diagnosis requiring progressive UMN+LMN dysfunction, improving sensitivity over El Escorial
notes: Shefner et al 2020. Gold Coast criteria: (1) progressive motor impairment, (2) UMN and LMN signs, (3) absence of alternative explanation. Replaces complex El Escorial certainty levels.

claim: Elevated neurofilament light chain (NfL) is a sensitive diagnostic and prognostic biomarker in ALS, with higher levels predicting faster progression
notes: Benatar et al 2023, Lancet Neurology. NfL elevated in presymptomatic carriers and at diagnosis. Level correlates with progression rate. Erik's NfL 5.82 (elevated) consistent with ALS diagnosis and moderate-fast progression risk.

claim: Sporadic ALS is biologically heterogeneous, with distinct molecular subtypes requiring different therapeutic strategies
notes: Emerging consensus from transcriptomic, proteomic, and clinical clustering studies. Not all sporadic ALS is identical — glial-predominant, oxidative, excitotoxic, and proteostatic subtypes may exist. Important for treatment stratification.

claim: TARDBP mutations account for ~3% of familial ALS and confirm TDP-43 as causative, not just a bystander, in ALS pathogenesis
notes: Sreedharan et al 2008, Science. Discovery of TARDBP mutations in familial ALS. Proved TDP-43 dysfunction is sufficient to cause ALS, not merely a downstream marker.

claim: GWAS identifies common risk variants (UNC13A, C21orf2, MOBP, SCFD1) contributing to sporadic ALS susceptibility
notes: van Rheenen et al 2016, Nicolas et al 2018. UNC13A SNP rs12973192 is a major sporadic ALS risk variant — now understood to modulate TDP-43-dependent cryptic exon severity.
